behaviors.description: Only files changed in the pull request are analyzed — unchanged files are not scanned
behaviors.justification: The real failure is: code ignores the GitHub API response listing changed files and analyzes the entire repo, or parses the changed-files list incorrectly. A Unit test of the filtering logic in isolation (mocking GitHub API) could pass while the integration between API call and file selection fails. We need Integration level to verify: (1) GitHub API is called to fetch changed files, (2) the response is correctly parsed, (3) only those files are passed to the linter. This requires a realistic multi-file scenario where some files changed and some didn't.
behaviors.test_description: Set up a filesystem with 10 test files. Mock GitHub API to report only 5 files changed. Run the CI lint step. Verify the linter only analyzed the 5 changed files and did not touch the 5 unchanged files. Track which files were passed to the linter subprocess.

behaviors.description: Findings are grouped by severity (error, warning, info) in the posted PR comment
behaviors.justification: The failure mode has two parts: (1) grouping logic broken, (2) grouping logic correct but posting code doesn't use it. A Unit test of just the grouping function could pass while the posted comment shows ungrouped findings. To catch the full failure—'findings are grouped in the posted comment'—we must test that grouped findings actually appear in the PR comment text. This requires testing the integration between (a) linter results, (b) grouping logic, (c) markdown formatting, and (d) the GitHub API call. We need to mock GitHub but verify the exact comment payload.
behaviors.test_description: Mock linter to return 6 findings: 2 errors, 2 warnings, 2 infos. Capture the comment payload sent to GitHub API. Verify the comment text has three sections (Error, Warning, Info), each containing the correct findings in order, with no intermingling of severity levels.

behaviors.description: A pull request that touches 200 files completes analysis within the 10-minute Actions timeout
behaviors.justification: The real failure is timeout—a real-time performance boundary. Mocked linters (or pure Unit tests) run instantly and hide slow code. We cannot catch this failure without running the actual linter on real files and measuring elapsed time. However, we don't need real GitHub Actions or a real PR; we need local file I/O and actual linter subprocess execution with realistic file content to measure true performance.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temporary directory with 200 test files (realistic size/content for the linter). Mock GitHub API to report all 200 changed. Run the lint step. Record wall-clock time from start to finish. Assert elapsed time < 10 minutes. Verify all 200 files were analyzed (not just a sample).

behaviors.description: When the linter process crashes on a malformed file, the remaining files are still analyzed and results posted
behaviors.justification: The failure is subprocess crash and recovery—a real OS behavior. To catch it, the code must handle real subprocess.CalledProcessError, exit codes, or signal termination. A Unit mock that simulates a crash exception might not accurately represent real subprocess behavior (partial output before crash, stderr content, signal handling). We need the actual linter subprocess to fail on a real malformed file while other subprocesses (or loop iterations) continue. This is a subprocess/OS-level behavior test.
behaviors.test_description: Create 5 test files: file 1 is malformed (will crash linter), files 2–5 are valid. Mock GitHub API to report all 5 changed. Run the lint step. Verify: (1) the linter crashed on file 1, (2) the step did not abort, (3) files 2–5 were analyzed and findings posted, (4) results were posted to GitHub despite the crash.

behaviors.description: Duplicate findings on the same line from overlapping rules are deduplicated in the output
behaviors.justification: Deduplication is pure data-manipulation logic—a local function boundary. The real failure is the deduplication logic is broken or missing (e.g., code doesn't call it, or calls it with wrong parameters). No external infrastructure is needed; no process crash or timeout is involved. A Unit test with mocked linter results (returning duplicate findings) can fully exercise and verify the deduplication logic. Mocking won't hide this failure because it's not an integration or infrastructure issue.
behaviors.test_description: Mock linter to return findings: [Error on line 5 from rule A, Error on line 5 from rule B, Warning on line 5 from rule A]. Pass to deduplication logic. Verify output contains exactly 2 findings (one Error, one Warning on line 5); the duplicate (Error on line 5 from rule A or B) is removed.
